topic: An artist discovers someone has tattooed their work
raw_output:
[story 1]

The turpentine fumes were thick, clinging to my throat like damp velvet. I stared at the canvas, the vibrant chaos of the seascape suddenly mocking me. A sharp prickle ran down my spine, a feeling like static electricity building just beneath my skin. I leaned closer, my breath catching in my chest. There, nestled in the corner of the painted wave, was a tiny, perfect depiction of my own signature—a delicate, intricate tattoo. My hands began to tremble, the brush slipping from my grasp. Who? How could this be? The world seemed to tilt, the familiar angles of the studio warping into something unstable. I backed away slowly, my eyes wide, searching the room for an explanation that simply wasn't there.

[story 2]

I kept rubbing my temples, a dull ache throbbing behind my eyes. The light in the loft felt too harsh, too sharp, slicing through the dust motes dancing in the air. When I finally looked at the portrait I’d just finished—the one about fractured memories—a cold dread settled in my stomach. A line of ink, impossibly fine, traced the curve of the subject's neck, mirroring the very pattern I’d sketched in my mind hours before. My jaw tightened. I stood up abruptly, knocking over a jar of pigment. Everything felt strangely distant, like watching a film through warped glass. I walked to the window, pressing my forehead against the cool glass, trying to anchor myself to something solid, but the ground seemed to shift beneath my feet.

[story 3]

My thoughts were a jumble of disconnected fragments, like shattered pieces of ceramic scattered across the floor. I picked up the charcoal stick, but my fingers felt clumsy, unresponsive. Then I saw it. On the background of my latest piece, a subtle, almost hidden symbol—a stylized raven in flight. It was undeniably a tattoo, the precise shading and depth unmistakable. A strange hollowness opened up in my chest, a vacuum where certainty used to reside. I paced the narrow space between the easel and the drying racks, my movements jerky and uncoordinated. I kept catching my reflection in the polished metal of the frame, and for a split second, I couldn't recognize the person staring back.
